---
title: "Pinebarren Aster (Oclemena reticulata)"
slug: "oclemena-reticulata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474887369/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Pinebarren Aster"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474887369/medium.jpg" alt="Pinebarren Aster" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/saherron">
saherron</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264455551">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=126701)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon across Florida.

## Natural history notes

A perennial wildflower characterized by its branching stems and numerous small, pale purple to pinkish flower heads with yellow centers. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as flatwoods, savannas, and the edges of wetlands. It typically blooms in late summer through fall and provides a nectar source for a variety of pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/126701-Oclemena-reticulata)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2355)
